title: Monitoring Performance in the Wild
concept: Monitoring a deployed model means tracking its predictions and input patterns over time to catch drift early, even before you receive confirmed correct answers.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What's the key difference between data drift and concept drift?

options.text: Data drift shifts input values while the rule holds; concept drift breaks the rule itself.
Data drift only affects classification models.
Concept drift only happens once per year.

exercises.explain: Correct! Data drift = inputs shift, rule holds. Concept drift = the rule itself breaks.

cards.title: Checking your car's dashboard, not just the engine
cards.body: You don't need to tear apart your car's engine to know something's wrong — a flashing warning light on the dashboard tells you early. Monitoring a deployed model works the same way: we watch signals that hint at trouble, long before we get final proof.

cards.title: Production Monitoring
cards.body: Monitoring is the ongoing practice of tracking a live model's inputs and outputs after deployment. Since we often don't know the true answer immediately (e.g. did this loan actually default?), we watch proxy signals instead.

cards.title: A thermometer for your model
cards.body: Think of monitoring like taking a patient's temperature regularly, without needing a full diagnosis every time. If the model's prediction patterns suddenly spike or shift, it's like a fever — a signal that something is wrong, even before you know the exact cause.

cards.title: What we actually watch
cards.body: Teams track: 1. **Input distribution shifts** (are today's ages/incomes wildly different from training data?), 2. **Prediction distribution shifts** (is the model suddenly predicting 'fraud' 10x more often than usual?), 3. **Confidence scores** (is the model suddenly much less certain about its guesses?).

cards.title: You don't always need instant ground truth
cards.body: You might think you can only monitor a model once you know the real answer (e.g. waiting months to see if a loan defaulted). In reality, tracking input and output patterns gives an early warning system, letting teams catch problems long before the final outcomes are confirmed.

cards.title: What Gets Monitored
cards.body: Signals watched in production, without needing instant ground truth:

left.text:
Feature value ranges.
Missing value rates.
New unseen categories.

right.text:
Prediction class balance.
Confidence score trends.
Sudden spikes in one label.

exercises.prompt: Why do teams monitor a model's input and output patterns even before they know the true correct answers?

options.text: To get an early warning of potential drift, long before ground truth confirmation is available.
Because ground truth is never needed for machine learning models.
To automatically retrain the model every second.
To reduce the size of the model file.

exercises.explain: Correct! Monitoring input/output patterns gives an early signal before final outcomes are confirmed.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A sudden spike in how often a model predicts one particular class can be an early warning sign of drift.
exercises.explain: True! Unexpected shifts in the prediction distribution are a classic early monitoring signal.

exercises.sentence: Tracking a live model's input and output patterns after deployment is called ___.
exercises.explain: Monitoring is the ongoing tracking of a deployed model's behavior.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a proxy signal a team could use to detect early drift, without waiting for confirmed ground truth?

options.text: The model's average prediction confidence score has dropped from 92% to 61% over the past week.
The model file size on disk has increased by 2 megabytes.

exercises.explain: Correct! A drop in average confidence scores is a strong proxy signal of potential drift.

exercises.prompt: A fraud detection model normally flags about 2% of transactions as fraudulent. This week, monitoring dashboards show it's suddenly flagging 15% of transactions. The team hasn't yet confirmed which flags are real fraud. What should they do?

options.text: Treat the spike as an early drift warning signal and investigate immediately, without waiting for full ground truth confirmation.
Ignore it completely since ground truth hasn't been confirmed yet.
Immediately delete the model since it must be broken.
Assume fraud has genuinely increased 7.5x and do nothing.

exercises.explain: Exactly! A sudden 7.5x jump in flagged predictions is a major proxy signal worth investigating right away — that's the entire point of monitoring. Next, let's learn what teams actually do once they detect this kind of drift!
